Core Mechanics and Rules

MCC runs on an OSR chassis compatible with Dungeon Crawl Classics: ability modifiers, swingy d20 tests, critical hits, and fumbles. Character funnels send zero‑level peasants into the meatgrinder; a handful survive to become classed adventurers (Sentinel, Shaman, Rover, etc.). Mutations and artifacts function as wildcard powers—impactful but risky—encouraging creative problem‑solving over set piece builds. Judges lean on rulings and reaction rolls, while players husband torches, rations, and hit points like their lives depend on it—because they do.

What Makes It Unique

Where many post‑apocalyptic games skew grim, MCC embraces weird science fantasy: psychic flora, rad‑zones, and forgotten satellites that still answer to garbled commands. Its robust adventure support—hexes, ruin crawls, and tournament modules—makes it easy to drop a mission on the table and play tonight. The artifact usage flow (identify → risk → master) turns every discovery into a mini‑game, and mutation tables guarantee surprises without bogging play.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Ideal for tables that enjoy OSR lethality, rulings‑over‑rules, and exploration as the core loop. New groups can onboard quickly via the funnel, while veterans get deep campaign legs from hexcrawls and village‑level consequences. If you want pulpy survival with big dangers, bigger swings, and constant discovery, MCC delivers.

related.title: Dungeon Crawl Classics
related.description: If you like MCC’s lethal funnels and swingy OSR combat, Dungeon Crawl Classics offers the fantasy counterpart—same dice conventions, gonzo modules, and push‑your‑luck spellcasting, but without the post‑apoc scavenging focus.

related.title: Mutant: Year Zero
related.description: Shares survival focus, resource scarcity, and hexcrawl exploration, but uses a modern d6 pool with push mechanics and base‑building, while MCC sticks to OSR swing, classes, and funnels.

related.title: Forbidden Lands
related.description: Also emphasizes travel hazards and survival; Forbidden Lands is grittier and procedural with Year Zero mechanics, whereas MCC leans pulpy, mutation‑forward dungeon raids.

data.title: Alone Among the Stars
data.review_summary: A calm, minimalist solo journaling game praised for ease of entry and evocative prompts; common caveats mention wanting more tables and variety over long plays.
data.blurb: Alone Among the Stars is a rules‑lite solo journaling game about exploring strange worlds. Using a standard deck of cards and a few dice, you discover prompts and write short vignettes about what you find. Classless and ultra‑low prep, it focuses on reflective, creative play. Ideal for quiet sessions and writing warm‑ups.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Solo journaling • Cards + d6 • 1 player • Zero prep • Rules‑lite • 15–45 min sessions

Theme and Setting

You are a lone traveler crossing vast, uncharted space. Each new planet offers a mood piece rather than a mapped location—glimpses of flora, weather, ruins, and fleeting encounters sketched through a handful of prompts. The tone is contemplative, sometimes melancholic, with wonder and solitude at its core.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Play uses a standard 52‑card deck and a six‑sided die. Draw a card to determine a category (terrain, life, artifacts, phenomena), then roll to refine the prompt. You respond by writing a few sentences—a snapshot or journal entry. There are no stats to track or combats to resolve; the engine is your imagination guided by constraints.

Sessions are modular and quick: visit a new world, make several discoveries, then depart. The rules fit on a few pages and can be memorized after a single read, making it easy to return to whenever inspiration strikes.

What Makes It Unique

The structure elegantly balances randomness with authorial choice. The limited but flavorful prompts push you past blank‑page anxiety while leaving ample room for tone and genre tweaks. It’s endlessly hackable—many designers cite it as a foundation for their own solo games—and it pairs well with ambient music or art for vibe‑driven play.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Perfect for solo players, writers, and GMs looking to spark ideas between sessions. If you want low‑pressure, creative play with zero bookkeeping, this sings. It’s also a great entry point for folks new to solo RPGs: approachable, replayable, and satisfying in short bursts.

related.title: The Wretched
related.description: The Wretched is a bleaker, time‑pressured solo experience using a tumbling block tower; compared to AAtS, it’s tense survival horror rather than reflective exploration.

related.title: A Quiet Year
related.description: Community‑focused map‑drawing that trades solitude for collaborative worldbuilding; use it when you want endurance and scarcity to shape a settlement over seasons.

related.title: Ironsworn
related.description: A robust solo/coop quest engine with progress tracks and moves; choose it when you want longer‑form survival journeys with stakes and advancement beyond vignette play.

data.title: Best Left Buried
data.review_summary: Reviewers highlight a fast, focused chassis that makes hard choices constant—light, load, and fear all press in. Praise centers on mood, tables, and the elegant stress/scar loop; common caveats note its intentionally lethal tone and preference for rulings over exhaustive rules.
data.blurb: Best Left Buried is a rules‑lite fantasy horror about desperate cryptdiggers braving lethal, resource‑starved dungeons. Using simple d20 tests and three core attributes, play moves fast while stress, scars, and dwindling light drive tense survival choices. Classless, low‑prep, and exploration‑driven—ideal for gritty crawls and short, high‑stakes sessions.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: OSR/NSR horror • d20 saves/tests • 3–5 + GM • Low prep • Rules‑lite survival • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

Best Left Buried frames delving as grim, precarious work. Cryptdiggers venture into lightless vaults, picking through eerie architecture, unreliable maps, and omens. The tone is oppressive and uncanny rather than splatter—fear, exhaustion, and bad luck are as dangerous as any monster. Settlements, caravans, and hirelings offer momentary safety, but every expedition threatens to leave bodies or minds changed.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Rules are deliberately lean: three attributes resolve most tests with a simple roll, with advantage/disadvantage–style modifiers and equipment mattering at the table. Resource tracking—torches, rations, fatigue, and encumbrance—creates constant trade‑offs. Stress accrues from fear and calamity, converting into lasting Scars that reshape abilities and outlook. Monsters are written for mood and danger first; their mechanics stay light to keep focus on choices, consequences, and pacing.

What Makes It Unique

The game marries OSR‑style crawl discipline (mapping, logistics, lethal stakes) with psychological pressure. Instead of power curves, characters accumulate stories: scars, superstitions, and compromises that mark survival. Modules emphasize readable procedures and evocative tables over rigid stat blocks, making it effortless to improvise crypt hazards, hireling foibles, and strange treasures at the table.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Great for groups who enjoy tense exploration, meaningful resource decisions, and horror that comes from atmosphere and attrition. Newcomers can grasp it quickly, while veterans will appreciate how little prep is needed to produce memorable, high‑pressure sessions. Expect short arcs of grim triumph, hard bargains, and the occasional desperate retreat—survival is the point, not heroism.

related.title: Into the Odd
related.description: Shares minimalist procedures and hazardous expeditions; BLB leans darker horror and stress/scars, while ItO emphasizes brisk combat and emergent oddities.

related.title: The Black Hack (2nd Edition)
related.description: Both deliver fast OSR play; BLB centers dread, logistics, and lasting Scars, while TBH streamlines classic fantasy with universal d20 rolls.

related.title: Mörk Borg
related.description: Both embrace lethal, doom‑laden adventures; BLB is crawl‑focused survival horror with stress, while MB pushes swingy chaos and apocalyptic metal vibes.

data.title: The One Ring (Second Edition)
data.review_summary: Praise centers on its evocative journey rules, fellowship focus, and faithful Tolkien tone. Some note moderate crunch in travel/corruption subsystems and the need for spotlight balance across cultures, but overall it delivers thematic, low‑magic adventure with strong support for long campaigns.
data.blurb: The One Ring (Second Edition) is a Tolkien fantasy RPG built around journeys, weariness, and hope in the twilight of the Third Age. Classless and exploration‑driven, it emphasizes travel challenges, shadow risk, and fellowship phases over build minutiae. Ideal for story‑led campaigns and grounded heroic play.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Tolkien fantasy • Journey/Travel rules • 2–5 + Loremaster • Moderate prep • Medium‑light complexity • 2–4h sessions

Theme and Setting

Set in the twilight years of the Third Age, The One Ring centers ordinary heroes struggling against the slow return of the Shadow. Adventures emphasize the land itself—crossing wild places, seeking lost lore, and preserving small hopes in the face of ancient threats. Magic is rare and subtle; cultures, oaths, and hospitality matter more than spells.

Core Mechanics and Rules

The game uses a custom dice pool (d12 Feat die + d6 Success dice), where traits and gear add d6s and great/extraordinary successes add tangible effects. The standout system is the Journey: fatigue, terrain, and mishaps accumulate as roles (Guide, Hunter, Look‑out, Scout) confront hazards. Fellowship and Shadow subsystems model hope, weariness, and corruption, with distinct phases for adventure and rest/campaign upkeep.

What Makes It Unique

Travel is not a montage but a core challenge. Journeys produce meaningful consequences that shape subsequent encounters and social scenes. Advancement favors character depth, culture, and virtues over mechanical bloat; the rules keep combat readable while foregrounding peril from the road, the weather, and the will of the Shadow.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Best for tables seeking grounded, literary fantasy with a survival edge—navigating scarcity, fatigue, and moral strain rather than spell‑nova problem‑solving. Campaign tools support months of play; the system remains approachable once dice icons are learned. Ideal for groups that enjoy exploration, fellowship downtime, and tough journey choices.

related.title: Alien
related.description: Shares travel stress, scarcity, and lethal sci‑fi peril; Alien skews horror one‑shots, while TOR sustains long‑arc, low‑magic journeys with cultural bonds at the center.

related.title: Mothership
related.description: Both heighten attrition and panic under pressure; Mothership is faster and deadlier, TOR is steadier with hope/Shadow economy and robust downtime between expeditions.

related.title: Forbidden Lands
related.description: Hex‑crawl survival in both; Forbidden Lands leans OSR campcraft and strongholds, TOR leans structured journeys and culture‑driven virtues in Tolkien’s world.

data.title: Enter the Survival Horror
data.review_summary: Early readers highlight tight, high‑tension pacing, tactical choices under pressure, and an approachable FitD chassis. Some note that stress/clock management adds a bit of rules familiarity up front, but it pays off with cinematic survival‑horror beats and clear player agency.
data.blurb: Enter the Survival Horror is a rules‑lite, Forged in the Dark survival‑horror game about dark corridors, dwindling resources, and mounting dread. Classless and low‑prep, it frames tense exploration and tactical fights around a Doom Clock and clever inventory gambits. Ideal for one‑shots and short, nerve‑wracking campaigns.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Forged in the Dark • d6 dice pools • 2–5 + GM • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

Enter the Survival Horror aims squarely at classic survival‑horror: dim hallways, creeping monstrosities, and the cold math of deciding whether a single shell is worth the risk. Locations are tight and dangerous—police stations, derelict labs, quarantined towns—where exploration, backtracking, and route mastery are as important as combat. The tone leans cinematic and grim without being hopeless, keeping pressure high while spotlighting player ingenuity.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Built on a streamlined Forged in the Dark chassis, players roll small pools of d6s to resolve actions. Position and effect set the risk, while clocks track threats, progress, and looming catastrophe. A signature Doom Clock escalates stakes as time passes, tightening resources and spawning new dangers. Combat is lethal by design: Focus turns each encounter into a puzzle of timing, distance, and tool use rather than attrition. The game embraces inventory tension—Lucky Find lets you declare a needed item at a cost—so decisions about what to carry, spend, or risk are always meaningful.

What Makes It Unique

Two standout levers shape play. First, impending doom is made mechanical: clocks fill, lights go out, corridors become unsafe, and foes evolve. Survival is earned through planning and route knowledge, not just bigger numbers. Second, the “voices in your head” concept models intuition and doubt—your mental stats talk back, offering guidance with their own agendas. Combined with safety tools and catastrophe outcomes instead of hard character death, the system keeps the horror sharp while preserving player agency.

Target Audience and Player Experience

If you love Resident Evil‑style problem‑solving, limited ammo, and map mastery, this delivers that loop at the table with minimal prep. It’s classless and quick to teach, making it perfect for one‑shots, short campaigns, and actual play. GMs get crisp procedures for setting threat levels, seeding keys and shortcuts, and dialing tension with clocks. Players who enjoy creative risk‑taking, teamwork under pressure, and fiction‑first tactics will find a toolbox that rewards clever plans over stat optimization.

related.title: Mothership
related.description: Mothership dials horror to industrial‑sci‑fi and ups lethality; if EtSH is about route mastery and cinematic tension, Mothership leans into panic, stress, and brutal triage aboard failing ships.

related.title: Death in Space
related.description: Death in Space shares resource scarcity and bleak vibes, but trades FitD dice pools for minimalist d20 procedures—excellent if you want survival tension with even lighter rules.

related.title: The Wretched
related.description: The Wretched distills survival horror into a solo experience of isolation and dwindling hope; pair it with EtSH when you want to explore the genre’s quiet, internal terror.

data.title: Twilight: 2000 (Fourth Edition)
data.review_summary: Critics praise the boxed set's rich sandbox support, robust hexcrawl tools, and tense, gear-focused combat. Common caveats note higher rules weight than typical story games and a bleak tone that may not suit every table.
data.blurb: Twilight: 2000 is a post‑apocalyptic survival RPG built on a refined Year Zero Engine. Military‑leaning, sandbox play emphasizes hexcrawling, resource scarcities, and hard choices in a shattered 2000. Best for gritty campaigns about survival, rebuilding, and the costs of war.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Post‑Apocalyptic • Year Zero Engine • 2–5 + GM • Low‑to‑moderate prep • Medium complexity • 2–4h sessions

Theme and Setting

Set in an alternate year 2000 where World War III shattered Europe, Twilight: 2000 is about surviving the aftermath—scrounging for supplies, navigating fractured factions, and deciding whether to keep moving or stake a claim. The tone is grounded and military‑leaning but leaves room for civilian stories, local politics, and the fragile hope of rebuilding. The default Polish theater evokes ruined towns, contested roads, and tense encounters, with tools to rebase the campaign anywhere.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Built on a heavily adapted Year Zero Engine, the system uses attribute + skill dice pools with pushed rolls, criticals, and stress from scarcity and combat. The boxed set leans into hexcrawl procedures: overland travel, shifts, encounters, weather, and limited ammo and rations. Combat is fast but punishing—cover, positioning, and suppressive fire matter more than min‑maxed builds. A lightweight base‑building layer ties sessions together, letting groups fortify safehouses, workshops, and farms.

What Makes It Unique

Twilight: 2000 blends old‑school open‑world procedures with modern clarity. It’s not just firefights; it’s logistics as drama—tracking bullets, fuel, and injuries makes every route choice meaningful. The hex map, modular battle mats, and encounter cards make prep practical for long campaigns. The rules sit at a medium crunch point: richer than story‑first survival games, leaner than simulationist milsims. It rewards cautious play, local alliances, and creative problem‑solving.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Ideal for tables that enjoy gritty survival decisions, emergent travel play, and military‑realist flavor. Newcomers to Year Zero will find the core loop approachable, but expect more bookkeeping than ultra‑lite titles. Groups that liked Mutant: Year Zero or Forbidden Lands and want harsher, modern‑era stakes will feel at home. Campaigns shine when characters balance personal goals against the group’s dwindling stores—and decide what, and who, is worth saving.

related.title: Mutant: Year Zero
related.description: Shares Year Zero Engine roots and hexcrawl survival, but shifts to mutant wastelands and community building over military realism.

related.title: Forbidden Lands
related.description: Fantasy cousin with OSR‑flavored travel and site‑based exploration—less gear realism, more dungeon‑ruin expeditions and folklore dangers.

related.title: The Walking Dead Universe
related.description: Focuses on character drama and scarcity against zombies; mechanically lighter and narrative‑driven compared to T2K’s tactical grit.

data.title: Stay Frosty
data.review_summary: A fast OSR one-shot game of space marines under pressure. Reviews highlight quick prep, punchy Black Hack–style rules, and a tense ammo-and-stress loop; common caveats: it is lethal by design and tuned for missions over campaigns.
data.blurb: Stay Frosty is a rules‑lite military sci‑fi game using streamlined OSR/Black Hack–style rolls. Classless, low‑prep missions focus on tight objectives, dwindling resources, and squad tactics under pressure. Ideal for one‑shots or short arcs where survival and decisive action matter.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: OSR/Black Hack lineage • d20 tests with Advantage/Disadvantage • 3–5 + GM • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

Futuristic bug hunts, derelict ships, and corporate black ops. You play hardened marines facing overwhelming threats with limited gear and no backup.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Simple d20 tests with Advantage/Disadvantage, escalating tension clocks, and resource pressure on ammo and wounds. Character creation is fast and classless with specialty gear.

What Makes It Unique

Combines OSR lethality with mission‑driven objectives and panic‑inducing attrition. Emphasizes team positioning, suppressive fire, and evac timing over build math.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Perfect for Alien‑style survival and 80s military‑sci‑fi action. Great for convention slots, pick‑up nights, and groups who want fast resolution and high stakes.

related.title: Mothership
related.description: Shares lethal sci‑fi survival and stress management; Stay Frosty is lighter and faster for one‑shots, while Mothership supports longer campaigns and deeper subsystems.

related.title: Death in Space
related.description: Both deliver grimy space survival with failing tech; Death in Space leans toward scavenging and cosmic dread, Stay Frosty toward military missions and fireteam tactics.

related.title: Alien
related.description: Alien offers cinematic stress dice and franchise horror; Stay Frosty strips it to OSR bones for speed, trading branded depth for quick setup and immediate action.

data.title: Stoneburner
data.review_summary: Players praise Stoneburner’s approachable, rules‑lite Breathless engine and strong theme, highlighting fast character creation, satisfying base‑building, and tense resource decisions. Common notes: swingy step‑die attrition can punish overlong delves; shines in short, purposeful expeditions.
data.blurb: Stoneburner is a rules‑lite sci‑fantasy survival game using the Breathless step‑die engine. Solo‑friendly and classless kits drive quick, low‑prep play focused on demon‑haunted delves, resource juggling, and colony growth. Ideal for short, high‑stakes sessions where choices about light, tools, and risk define whether your dwarves rebuild—or burn.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: OSR/NSR‑inspired • Step‑die (Breathless) • 1–5 (solo‑friendly) • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 1–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

Stoneburner drops you into the Long Belt: cursed asteroid mines where House Grandrock’s legacy is equal parts debt, demons, and dwindling hope. You play a tight‑knit crew of space dwarves tasked with reclaiming galleries, salvaging relics, and keeping a fragile settlement alive. It’s sci‑fantasy with grime under the nails—part Deep Rock Galactic, part Dwarf Fortress, with a vein of doom‑hunting heroics.

Core Mechanics and Rules

The game runs on Breathless, a rules‑lite step‑die engine that models exhaustion and risk. Actions use dice that degrade (d10 → d8 → d6 → d4) as the delve wears on; careful resupply and clever play restore your edge. Kits (like Striker, Sinker, Spellwinder, Stanchion) give immediate identity without build crunch. Procedures cover delving, crafting, looting, and community management, keeping turns snappy and fiction‑first. Player‑facing rolls, clear clocks and tracks, and simple opposition make it ideal for solo or GM‑light tables.

What Makes It Unique

Stoneburner marries tense survival play with tangible colony‑building. Expeditions push deeper for ore and answers, then return to invest in workshops, defenses, and amenities that shape future dives. The loop rewards prudence: light, tools, and time are precious; pushing your luck invites calamity. The demon ecology and mine generators offer strong prompts without locking you to prewritten plots, making each shaft feel discovered rather than designed.

Target Audience and Player Experience

If you want quick, punchy sessions with meaningful consequences, this sings. Solo delvers get clean guidance for oracles and procedures; groups get emergent objectives and shared risk management. New players can grasp the kit‑forward characters in minutes, while veterans can savor route‑planning, loadouts, and settlement tradeoffs. It’s perfect for short campaigns and one‑shots that feel complete—salvage a gallery, stave off a crisis, upgrade a district, and feel the settlement breathe again.

related.title: Mothership
related.description: Harder sci‑fi survival with stress and panic; deeper lethality and procedure, less colony‑building between dives.

related.title: Death in Space
related.description: Gritty, minimalist space decay—more cosmic rust and faction scum; fewer management layers than Stoneburner.

related.title: Ironsworn: Starforged
related.description: Solo‑first vow‑driven exploration—richer narrative tools and oracles; crunchier than Breathless but similarly expedition‑minded.

data.title: Into the Dungeon: Revived
data.review_summary: Players praise Into the Dungeon: Revived for its clean OSR presentation, fast character creation, and emphasis on risky exploration with meaningful resource pressure. Common notes highlight its lethal combat and rulings-forward style—great for tense delves but less suited to tactical optimization.
data.blurb: Into the Dungeon: Revived is a rules‑lite OSR fantasy game using simple d20 saves and inventory‑driven resource play. Classless and low‑prep, it focuses on tense, survival‑minded dungeon and wilderness exploration with fast, lethal combat. Ideal for quick sessions, open tables, and sandboxes that reward caution and clever play.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: OSR/NSR lineage • d20 saves, inventory stress • 2–5 + Warden/GM • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

Into the Dungeon: Revived embraces grimy, peril‑fraught fantasy where food, light, and nerve are as crucial as steel. It assumes open‑ended worlds—procedural dungeons, hostile wilderness, and opportunistic settlements—where survival is earned by restraint and ingenuity rather than character build math.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Rules are concise: ability‑based saves resolve danger, inventory slots and burdens model encumbrance, and exhaustion plus injuries impose real consequences. Combat is quick and deadly, encouraging preparedness, scouting, and retreat. Advancement comes from discoveries and clever play over grinding. The Warden (GM) adjudicates rulings with clear procedures for exploration, travel, light, and risk.

What Makes It Unique

ItDR distills classic dungeon‑delving into modern, readable procedures: slot‑based inventory for meaningful gear choices; straightforward saves that keep the pace snappy; and survival economics—torches, rations, and time—driving decisions. The text hews close to Into the Odd/Knave sensibilities while staying resolutely classless and toolkit‑friendly.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Perfect for groups who want fast start‑ups, lethal stakes, and campaign‑length sandboxes that feel dangerous but fair. New players can grasp the rules in minutes, while veterans will appreciate its clean OSR ergonomics. Expect tense delves, emergent solutions, and a premium on caution, mapping, and teamwork.

related.title: Into the Odd
related.description: Into the Odd leans even lighter with stripped‑down stats and arcane artifacts; pair it with ItDR when you want hazard‑driven delves with weirder, industrial‑odd vibes.

related.title: Electric Bastionland
related.description: Electric Bastionland keeps the Into the Odd engine but pivots to urban survival and debt‑driven expeditions; use ItDR when you prefer classic dungeons and frontier wilderness.

related.title: Knave
related.description: Knave shares classless OSR minimalism and slot inventory; ItDR reads closer to traditional modules and hexcrawls, while Knave emphasizes universal compatibility and kitbashing.

data.title: ARC: Doom Tabletop RPG
data.review_summary: Critics praise ARC for razor-tight, rules-lite play with a real-time Doomsday Clock that keeps tension high. Tables highlight fast onboarding, clear GM scaffolding, and memorable stakes; common notes mention that its timer and light crunch are tuned for one-shots and short arcs rather than months-long sims.
data.blurb: ARC is a rules‑lite, doom‑clock RPG about racing the apocalypse. Classless and low‑prep, it uses 2d6 risk rolls and a real‑time Doomsday Clock to drive hard choices and teamwork. Tight, fiction‑first scenes over crunch make it ideal for one‑shots and short, high‑tension campaigns.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Survival drama • 2d6 risk rolls • Real‑time Doomsday Clock • 2–5 + GM (solo possible) • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

ARC frames stories where the world will end unless your table acts with purpose. Dooms range from cosmic disasters to intimate, hometown heartbreaks. Tone is flexible—grim, hopeful, mythic—but always under pressure: time is scarce, resources are thin, and choices matter.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Resolution uses concise 2d6 risk rolls tied to Skills and Approaches; players describe how they act to shape odds and consequences. The signature Doomsday Clock ticks in real time, converting table minutes into mounting threat. Initiative is intent‑based and collaborative, keeping everyone engaged between rolls. Magic and techniques are flavorful with costs that bite. There are no deep feat trees—capability emerges from fiction, gear, and how you spend precious minutes.

What Makes It Unique

ARC makes time the core resource. The ticking clock shifts scenes from dithering to decisive: scouting, planning, and trade‑offs become dramatic because doom advances whether you act or not. Guidance and tools (dooms, omens, clocks, sheets) make it easy to facilitate even if you’re new to GMing.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Great for groups who enjoy survival stakes, quick onboarding, and story‑first play. Expect tense decisions, teamwork, and meaningful consequences in a tight 2–3 hour slot. If your table wants crunchy builds or tactical grids, ARC stays intentionally lean; if you want momentum and pressure that sings, this delivers.

related.title: Ten Candles
related.description: Both push fragile survival under a ticking limit; Ten Candles is tragic and ritual‑driven, while ARC leans action‑forward with a real‑time clock and broader genre range.

related.title: Dread
related.description: Each builds tension from risk and restraint: Dread’s tower externalizes fear, ARC’s Doomsday Clock externalizes time pressure; ARC supports longer arcs and more varied dooms.

related.title: Ironsworn
related.description: Both center perilous journeys and hard choices; Ironsworn emphasizes vows, progress tracks, and oracles for solo/coop, while ARC spotlights real‑time urgency and group coordination.

data.title: The End of the World: Zombie Apocalypse
data.review_summary: Reviews highlight a fast, cinematic focus on immediate threats, stress, and group dynamics over simulation. Fans praise the pick-up-and-play scenarios and character creation-as-yourself option, while noting limited long-term campaign support and swingy lethality that fits the premise but may shock new groups.
data.blurb: The End of the World: Zombie Apocalypse is a rules‑lite modern survival RPG using simple dice pools and stress tracks. Classless and low‑prep, it centers on tense, cinematic decisions, scavenging, and group dynamics under pressure. Ideal for one‑shots or short arcs where danger escalates fast and hard choices define the story.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Modern Survival • d6 dice pools • 3–6 incl. GM • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

A present‑day zombie outbreak upends normal life in hours. Food, water, and safety are scarce; trust is a resource. Play ordinary people thrust into crisis—neighbors, friends, or even yourselves—trying to get out, hole up, or help others before the dead surround you.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Resolution uses small dice pools built from attributes and skills; higher results beat the difficulty, with degrees of success and complications. Stress and trauma accumulate quickly, degrading effectiveness until characters break or adapt. Equipment is ad‑hoc: improvised weapons, scarce ammo, and bags full of questionable supplies. Rules favor rulings and momentum over tactical grids—fast calls, fast consequences.

What Makes It Unique

Focus is squarely on immediate, cinematic survival. The book provides multiple outbreak scenarios and escalation tracks so the situation worsens even if the party stands still. Play ‘as yourselves’ is supported out of the box for an instantly relatable hook, but you can also build archetypes quickly for repeat play.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Perfect for groups who want tense, high‑stakes sessions without extensive prep or character crunch. Expect fast scenes, hard choices, and a tone that rewards caution, ingenuity, and cooperation. Great as a convention one‑shot, a short survival mini‑series, or a palate‑cleanser between longer campaigns.

related.title: The Walking Dead Universe
related.description: The Walking Dead Universe shares grounded, modern survival and hard trade‑offs under relentless pressure, but uses a campaign‑ready engine with faction play—great if you want longer arcs after a few EotW one‑shots.

related.title: Outbreak: Undead (2nd Edition)
related.description: Outbreak: Undead 2e dials up simulation with inventory, risk ratings, and survival planning—crunchier than EotW, ideal when you want logistics to drive tension.

related.title: Enter the Survival Horror
related.description: Enter the Survival Horror emulates classic video‑game pacing with lean rules and resource scarcity—lighter than EotW and a great fit for tight, set‑piece sessions.

data.title: Don't Rest Your Head
data.review_summary: Reviewers praise its razor-focused design and tense pacing: simple dice pools create escalating pressure as exhaustion and madness mount. Sessions play fast and cinematic, but some note its intensity and narrow premise work best for short campaigns or one‑shots.
data.blurb: Don't Rest Your Head is a rules‑lite psychological survival horror about insomniacs who awaken to the Mad City. Using small dice pools, exhaustion and madness fuel risky power at the cost of control. Classless and low‑prep, it excels at high‑tension one‑shots and short arcs.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Psychological horror • d6 dice pools (Discipline/Exhaustion/Madness) • 2–5 + GM • Near‑zero prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

You can’t sleep. As insomnia pushes you past the edge, you slip into the Mad City—an uncanny, predatory reflection of our world ruled by Nightmares. You are one of the Awake: desperate, gifted, and hunted. Every hour you survive is a victory bought with fraying sanity.

Core Mechanics and Rules

The system runs on opposed d6 pools. You always roll Discipline; you may add Exhaustion dice to push harder and Madness dice to unleash dangerous talents. The highest die (and then majority) determines which pool dominates the outcome—steady Discipline, overtaxing Exhaustion, or chaotic Madness—each with narrative fallout.

Two economy tokens, Hope and Despair, pass between players and GM to bend results and heighten stakes. Exhaustion spirals toward collapse; Madness risks losing control to your Nightmare self. The rules are compact, teach in minutes, and keep scenes sharp and lethal.

What Makes It Unique

Play focuses on the tension between power and price. Pushing with Exhaustion guarantees competence now but drags you toward burnout; invoking Madness can solve impossible problems while inviting the Nightmares in. The game’s fiction and mechanics lock together to manufacture dread without heavy prep or complex subsystems.

Its one‑book format and strong GM guidance make it easy to run: frame scenes hard, escalate pressure, spotlight each character’s Scar and Madness talent, and keep clocks (literal and figurative) ticking.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Ideal for groups who want a visceral, cinematic horror night: quick to learn, intense at the table, and best in one‑shots or short arcs. Safety tools and tone calibration are recommended; the game leans bleak, surreal, and confrontational. If you enjoy Dread’s rising peril or Ten Candles’ inevitable doom but want mechanical push‑your‑luck dials, this hits the mark.

related.title: Dread
related.description: Like DRYH, Dread builds escalating tension, but it uses a Jenga tower instead of dice; DRYH’s dominance rolls give you finer control over when to risk collapse versus hold steady.

related.title: Ten Candles
related.description: Ten Candles scripts inevitable doom via dwindling light; DRYH offers a fighting chance if you gamble Exhaustion and Madness wisely—both deliver bleak, intimate horror with minimal prep.

related.title: The Wretched
related.description: The Wretched is solo survival via a tumbling tower and journaling; DRYH is group play with push‑your‑luck dice, trading journaling introspection for frenetic, shared desperation.

data.title: The Wildsea
data.review_summary: Early praise highlights the game’s gorgeous art and evocative setting. Reviewers note a moderate learning curve due to bespoke terminology, but praise the fiction-first flow once the crew and ship click. Common caveats include travel bookkeeping if overused, and that it skews narrative over tactical combat.
data.blurb: The Wildsea is a narrative survival-fantasy RPG set on an endless treetop ocean. Using a fast d6 pool with twists, crews sail chainsaw-prowed ships, scavenge, and survive weird hazards. Classless and highly creative, it favors low-prep, fiction-first play with strong setting prompts. Ideal for exploration-forward campaigns.
data.body_html:
At-a-glance: Post-apocalyptic fantasy • d6 dice-pool with twists • 2–6 incl. Firefly (GM) • Low-to-medium prep • Rules-medium • 2–4h sessions

Theme and Setting

The Verdancy drowned the world in mile-high trees, leaving only an endless canopy — the Wildsea. Crews of wildsailors cut paths through the rustling waves on chainsaw-prowed ships, trading, salvaging, and surviving among ports perched on tallshanks, floating spits, and treetop reefs. Flora and fauna are dangerous, beautiful, and often useful.

Core Mechanics and Rules

The Wildsea uses a fiction-first d6 pool. Combine an Edge, relevant Skills, and advantages to build a pool; highest single die sets the outcome (Triumph, Conflict, Disaster). Doubles create a Twist — a side effect that changes the scene. Progress Tracks, Journeys, and Montages pace travel, downtime, and long actions. Damage marks Aspects or inflicts Injuries; death is a narrative choice.

What Makes It Unique

Character options (bloodlines, origins, posts) and collaborative ship-building push creativity without heavy math. Resources like salvage, specimens, and whispers fuel crafting and improvisation. The setting is “low magic, high weirdness”: arconautics, living ships, leviathans, and crezzerin-laced biology drive play.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Great for groups that enjoy exploration, discovery, and vivid worldbuilding. The bespoke terms and travel procedures add texture but can slow play if overemphasized; many tables treat journeys as flavorful montages punctuated by sharp encounters. If you like Blades-style clocks with a wilder vibe, this will click.

related.title: Into the Odd
related.description: Shares fast lethality and exploration-first play, but Into the Odd is leaner and more OSR-structured; The Wildsea is stranger, with narrative twists and collaborative ship journeys.

related.title: Electric Bastionland
related.description: Both prize quick rulings and creative problem-solving. Bastionland focuses on urban delves and failed careers; The Wildsea widens to treetop sailing, resource crafting, and crew-driven voyages.

related.title: Death in Space
related.description: Each is gritty survival with salvage loops. Death in Space is bleak industrial sci-fi; The Wildsea is post-Verdancy fantasy with vivid biohazards and a more narrative resolution system.

data.title: Barbarians of the Ruined Earth
data.review_summary: A post-apocalyptic, Thundarr-inspired OSR game that plays fast and dirty. Reviews highlight straightforward Black Hack lineage, punchy classes, and gonzo encounters; common caveats mention swingy lethality and a focus on rulings over rules.
data.blurb: Barbarians of the Ruined Earth is a rules‑lite post‑apocalyptic sword‑and‑sorcery game built on The Black Hack. Class-based, low‑prep play emphasizes scavenging, exploration, and brutal, fast combat across a weird wasteland. Ideal for one‑shots or sandboxes where survival, resource management, and pulp action drive the night.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: OSR/Black Hack lineage • d20 checks/saves • 3–5 + GM • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

A blasted Earth of crystal deserts, mutant warlords, and ancient sorcery. Thundarr‑style pulp adventure meets scavenger survival as parties trek between perilous ruins and scrap‑built settlements.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Uses streamlined Black Hack mechanics: roll‑under ability tests, usage dice for resources, and quick, lethal combat. Classes provide distinct abilities without heavy build math; rulings over rules keep scenes moving.

What Makes It Unique

Gonzo post‑apocalyptic fantasy tone with pick‑up‑and‑play procedures for hex‑crawls, scavenging, and hazards. Supports emergent survival play—improvise solutions, conserve gear, and push luck against a hostile world.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Great for groups that want fast OSR action, memorable failures, and survival tension without dense crunch. New players onboard quickly; veterans get a sharp toolkit for sandboxes and convention one‑shots.

related.title: The Black Hack (2nd Edition)
related.description: Shares fast, rulings‑first OSR combat and lethal attrition; BotRE layers a gonzo wasteland tone on Black Hack’s quick chassis.

related.title: Cairn
related.description: Both emphasize lightweight survival exploration and resource pressure; Cairn skews woodland folk‑horror while BotRE goes pulp post‑apoc.

related.title: Best Left Buried
related.description: Each delivers quick, dangerous delves with mounting dread and tough choices; BLB leans grimdark horror, BotRE leans pulp mayhem.

data.title: HOSTILE
data.review_summary: Gritty, blue-collar sci‑fi built on Cepheus/2d6. Reviews consistently praise its cohesive rules, grounded tone, and lethal survival focus, with stress/panic and harsh environment procedures called out as highlights; common caveats note its utilitarian presentation and a moderate crunch vs. ultra‑lite systems.
data.blurb: HOSTILE is a near‑future, blue‑collar sci‑fi survival RPG using classic 2d6 task resolution. Classless and low‑prep, it emphasizes hazardous jobs, hard vacuum, and resource management over heroics. Ideal for tense one‑shots, salvage runs, and ongoing colony campaigns with a gritty, industrial vibe.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Retro‑future sci‑fi • 2d6 (Cepheus) • 2–5 + Referee • Low prep • Moderate complexity • 2–4h sessions

Theme and Setting

HOSTILE imagines a grim, utilitarian future shaped by off‑world extraction, corporate contracts, and dangerous frontier work. Life in the American Sector is precarious: colonies cling to marginal worlds, companies run lean, and crews of roughnecks, haulers, and scouts keep the lights on. Alien life, while rare, is hostile; the environment is worse.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Built on Cepheus/2d6, the rules use straightforward attribute + skill checks with target numbers and degrees of effect. Character creation supports both lifepath and non‑random options. Survival procedures cover vacuum, radiation, toxic atmospheres, heat, dehydration, and more; stress and panic rules escalate tension under pressure. Tactical combat leans lethal and practical (aimed fire, suppressive fire, vehicles), while ship operations emphasize checklists, procedures, and crew roles.

What Makes It Unique

Rather than heroics, HOSTILE centers blue‑collar problem solving under industrial constraints: time, tools, fatigue, and corporate policy. Campaign frames like haulage, prospecting, disaster response, and colony support provide mission‑driven structure. The setting’s tone—think Outland, Alien, and corporate memos—keeps play grounded and tense. It’s compatible with the broader Cepheus ecosystem, yet focused enough to feel purpose‑built for survival sci‑fi.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Great for groups who enjoy gritty logistics, crew drama, and procedural play. Newcomers to 2d6 can pick it up quickly; veterans will appreciate the survival depth and stress systems. One‑shots shine when you want a high‑stakes job gone sideways; campaigns reward long‑term ship upkeep, route learning, and colony politics.

related.title: Mothership
related.description: Shares lethal, blue‑collar space horror—HOSTILE skews procedural and corporate, while Mothership amps up panic‑driven dungeon‑crawl pacing and zine‑ready lethality.

related.title: Alien
related.description: Both deliver working‑class sci‑fi dread; Alien leans cinematic with strong scenario support, while HOSTILE favors open‑ended campaigns and crunchy survival procedures.

related.title: Death in Space
related.description: Rules‑lite, rust‑punk void grime vs. HOSTILE’s procedural Cepheus backbone—pick DiS for minimalist bleakness, HOSTILE for systems‑driven survival ops.

data.title: Never Going Home
data.review_summary: Fast to learn but unflinching in tone, Never Going Home delivers bleak, WWI trench survival with a tense card-hand resource loop. Groups praise quick character turnover and Unit-focused play that keeps momentum even as soldiers fall. Caveats: the attrition is deliberate, and Whispered powers trade short-term advantage for a slow bleed of humanity.
data.blurb: Never Going Home is a rules-lite WWI survival-horror game using straightforward tests plus card-hand pressure. Classless and low-prep, it follows a front-line Unit through lethal missions, dwindling supplies, and the corrupting Whispers. Ideal for short campaigns and players who enjoy grim stakes and tough choices.
data.body_html:
At-a-glance: WWI survival-horror • Dice + card hand management • 2–5 + GM • Low prep • Rules-lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

Never Going Home places your table in the mud, wire, and shellfire of the First World War, then tears a hole in reality. The Veil is open and alien Whispers seep into soldiers’ dreams, offering power at a cost. You play members of a front-line Unit trying to survive patrols, raids, and retreats while balancing duty, fear, and humanity. The tone is grim but grounded: supernatural horror is layered atop the authentic misery of trench warfare.

Core Mechanics and Rules

The system is streamlined for fast play. Characters are quick to create, with compact statlines and gear that emphasize role and readiness over build math. Resolution uses simple dice tests supported by a dwindling hand of cards—spent to push luck, absorb shocks, or seize fleeting advantages. The Unit is the focus: individual soldiers may be wounded, rotated out, or lost, but the story carries on through replacements and battlefield promotions.

Whispered abilities tempt you with potent effects—insight, resilience, or brutal force—paid for in fraying nerves and creeping transformation. Resource tracks stay lean: rations, ammunition, and morale matter, but bookkeeping never bogs down decisions at the table. Procedures for patrols, encounters, and after-action fallout keep sessions moving with minimal prep.

What Makes It Unique

Never Going Home threads survival gameplay through a war story where the party identity (the Unit) persists even as individual PCs come and go. The card-hand pressure system bakes attrition into every choice, mirroring the era’s exhaustion without heavy rules overhead. Horror escalates through visible costs rather than hidden complexity: every shortcut against the Veil leaves a mark.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Best for groups who want bleak survival with decisive, fiction-first choices—and who are comfortable with character loss as part of the narrative. It excels at short arcs and linked operations: two to three hours per mission, low prep for the GM, and clear prompts for consequences between sessions. If you enjoy resource-tight OSR play, WWI themes, or survival horror where every advantage has a price, this sits squarely in that lane.

related.title: Mothership
related.description: Mothership shares lethal, resource-tight survival but shifts to sci-fi panic—stress saves and panic cascades replace the card-hand pressure while keeping sessions fast and deadly.

related.title: Those Dark Places
related.description: Industrial sci-fi dread with simple tests and corporate grime; fewer supernatural bargains, more claustrophobic tension and blue-collar mystery aboard failing ships and stations.

related.title: The Wretched
related.description: Solo survival horror that distills the spiral into one doomed protagonist—tower pulls and prompts echo NGM’s attrition but in an introspective, single-survivor frame.

data.title: Survive the Night
data.review_summary: Reviewers highlight its approachable d6 rules, tight pacing, and how quickly new players grasp the danger‑driven loop. Praise centers on atmosphere and ease of running one‑shots; common caveats note swingy lethality and limited campaign support by design.
data.blurb: Survive the Night is a rules‑lite horror one‑shot built for fast, cinematic survival. Using a simple d6 system and genre‑archetype playbooks, it leans on tension, resource scramble, and tough choices over build math. Low‑prep and party‑friendly, it shines at campfire scares and convention tables where escaping alive is the win condition.
data.body_html:
Horror survival • d6 tests • 3–6 incl. Narrator • Near‑zero prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

Survive the Night is about desperate people in over their heads, trying to make it to sunrise. It embraces classic horror tropes—isolated roads, haunted woodlands, empty houses with too many doors—and hands the table the tools to stage those set pieces with minimal rules overhead. Tone is tense and cinematic rather than splatter‑for‑its‑own‑sake; the fear comes from narrowing options, frayed nerves, and the creeping realization that not everyone will get out.

Core Mechanics and Rules

The engine is a streamlined d6 system. Players roll small pools or single dice against straightforward targets, with situational advantages and disadvantages doing most of the work that other games offload to dense character builds. Archetype playbooks (the Camper, the Bookworm, the Leader, and so on) provide immediately legible abilities that telegraph genre roles without locking players into crunch. Resource pressure—limited light, dwindling improvised tools, and the clock—pushes decisions. When things go wrong (and they will), consequences escalate quickly, reinforcing a short, punchy play cycle ideal for one‑shots.

What Makes It Unique

Where many horror RPGs either sprawl into campaign engines or hinge on a single physical gimmick, Survive the Night threads the middle: all the focus on escalating dread and hard choices, none of the setup friction. It is tuned for tables that want to get from zero to peril in minutes. The book supports Narrators with scenario structure, pacing advice, and pre‑built characters so a convention slot or a last‑minute game night can still deliver a complete arc—rising tension, break‑point, chase to the exit—without rules lookups slowing the camera.

Target Audience and Player Experience

If you like teaching new players, running Halloween sessions, or spiking your regular campaign with a high‑stakes side story, this fits. The resolution model is transparent, so decisions feel consequential even when the dice swing; character sheets are readable at a glance; and the system foregrounds description over modifiers. Expect tight scenes, frequent cliffhangers, and an endgame that rewards clever retreats as much as heroics. It is not built for long campaigns, but it excels at exactly what it promises: one grim, memorable night.

related.title: Dread
related.description: Both aim for tense, fatalistic horror one‑shots. Dread’s Jenga tower externalizes rising dread; Survive the Night uses swingy d6 tests and resource pressure. If you want tactile suspense, pick Dread; for pure narrative speed, pick Survive the Night.

related.title: Ten Candles
related.description: Ten Candles guarantees a tragic end and uses extinguishing candles to pace scenes. Survive the Night offers a slim chance to escape if the table plays smart. Both emphasize tone; pick by whether you want inevitable doom or a razor‑thin path out.

related.title: Zombie World
related.description: Zombie World’s card‑driven engine supports ongoing survival drama; Survive the Night is laser‑focused on a single terrifying evening. Great pairing: use Survive the Night for the outbreak prologue, then jump to Zombie World for the long haul.

data.title: Zombie World
data.review_summary: Reviewers praise Zombie World for fast, cinematic survival play: card-driven moves, escalating scarcity, and hard choices that keep tension high. The community highlights its excellent campaign kits and GM support. Common caveats: it’s tuned for narrative stakes over simulation and assumes players lean into genre tropes.
data.blurb: Zombie World is a rules‑lite, card‑driven survival RPG about a community fighting to live through the zombie apocalypse. Classless and low‑prep, it emphasizes tough decisions, resource pressure, and messy relationships over build math. Ideal for one‑shots and ongoing survival drama where cooperation—and sacrifice—matter.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Modern survival‑horror • Card‑driven resolution • 3–6 incl. MC • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

Zombie World focuses on community‑scale survival—food, safety, and trust are scarce while the dead press in. You’ll defend a fragile enclave, scavenge dangerous ground, and weigh what (and who) is worth saving as scarcity and pressure mount. It leans grounded and human: relationships, hard bargains, and the cost of staying alive take center stage.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Instead of long character builds, playbooks and card decks handle most of the work. Character creation is fast and evocative—identities, pasts, and tensions emerge immediately. Resolution uses small card draws that produce clear outcomes and complications, pushing the fiction forward without rules drag. Campaign kits provide locations, threats, and arcs that keep prep minimal while ensuring rising stakes. The system is classless, with gear, circumstances, and choices shaping capability more than stats.

What Makes It Unique

Zombie World keeps survival pressure tight: scarcity clocks, escalating dangers, and social fallout drive decisions as much as combat. It is built to play tonight and continue next week—each session advances community projects, reveals fractures, and forces trade‑offs between safety and risk. The card‑driven engine keeps tension visible and pacing brisk, ideal for tables that want cinematic outcomes without tactical grid overhead.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Perfect for groups who want tense, low‑prep survival drama with quick onboarding. New players grasp it in minutes; veterans get a toolbox for sustained arcs about trust, sacrifice, and slim chances. Expect hard calls, desperate plans, and the creeping realization that survival may cost more than you thought.

related.title: The Walking Dead Universe
related.description: Both spotlight modern survival under relentless pressure. The Walking Dead Universe runs longer arcs with faction play and Year Zero dice; Zombie World stays card‑driven and lean, tuned for fast scenes and escalating social fallout at the enclave scale.

related.title: All Flesh Must Be Eaten
related.description: Classic zombie survival with simple d10 tests and remixable Deadworlds; Zombie World is lighter and more narrative, using cards to keep tension high and prep minimal—great when you want quick starts and community drama.

related.title: Escape from Dino Island
related.description: Cinematic, rules‑lite survival focused on escape and tense one‑shots; pair it with Zombie World when you want similar pressure but a campaign‑friendly engine about holding a community together.

data.title: Mountaintop Isolation
data.review_summary: Consensus highlights the escalating tension of tower pulls, the grounded survival prompts, and the compassionate safety tools. Pacing stays brisk with concise entries, though the emotional intensity and bleak outcomes won’t fit every mood. Strong for reflective solo play.
data.blurb: Mountaintop Isolation is a solo journaling survival game where you document your struggle to endure a blizzard trapped alone in a remote ski cabin. Using cards, dice, and a tumbling block tower, you balance dwindling resources against the creeping cold. Ideal for intimate, reflective solo play.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Solo survival journaling • Cards + d6 + tower • 1 player • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 45–120 min sessions

Theme and Setting

Mountaintop Isolation strands you alone in a remote ski cabin as a whiteout blizzard cuts off all contact. Food, fuel, and body heat ebb away while the storm howls and the creaking timbers close in. The game explores the intersection of environment and psyche: the cabin becomes both shelter and trap, while memories and regrets surface with the cold.

Core Mechanics and Rules

This is a Wretched & Alone–style solo journaling game. Each turn you draw from an oracle deck (a standard 52‑card deck), interpret prompts, roll occasional d6 checks, and pull from a tumbling block tower to push your luck as conditions worsen. Optional procedures replace the tower if you don’t have one. Prompts focus on survival pressures—fuel rationing, exposure, injury, hunger—and on internal responses like fear, paranoia, and determination. You record outcomes as short diary entries, voice logs, or bullet points.

Resolution is intentionally simple: the cards set the fiction, the die clarifies uncertainty, and the tower embodies mounting risk. Failure states are stark—worsening weather, broken equipment, dwindling supplies—while successes often cost time, calories, or composure. The rules encourage concise entries, so play stays brisk even as tension climbs.

What Makes It Unique

Rather than monster chases or combat, Mountaintop Isolation centers practical survival—heat, shelter, water, and safe descent—filtered through an intimate, reflective lens. The blizzard is the antagonist, and the tower captures the grind of attrition better than hit points. Safety notes and content warnings are foregrounded; you’re encouraged to pause, skip, or reframe prompts that don’t feel right in the moment.

The game’s focus on ordinary gear, small victories, and environmental hazards delivers grounded, credible survival fiction. Its modular approach to procedure means you can play at a desk with a tower, in a notebook with dice, or entirely digitally.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Ideal for fans of solo survival narratives, journaling games, and tension engines like Dread. Sessions fit well into 45–120 minutes, with a natural arc from assessment to improvisation to resolution. Newcomers can grasp the rules in minutes, and experienced solo players will appreciate how prompts layer resource management with emotional stakes.
data.notes: Survival;Solo Play;Rules Lite;Horror;Environmental;Low Prep;Quick-Play

related.title: The Wretched
related.description: Shares the same Wretched & Alone tension engine—tower pulls escalate risk while journaling frames isolation—but Mountaintop Isolation narrows the lens to realistic cold‑weather survival rather than sci‑fi wreckage and distress beacons.

related.title: Ten Candles
related.description: Also plays with dwindling light and inevitable outcomes, but Ten Candles is group‑based tragic horror; Mountaintop Isolation keeps the focus on solitary, practical survival choices under a blizzard’s pressure.

related.title: Dread
related.description: Both use a tumbling block tower to translate rising narrative stress into mechanical risk. Dread supports scenario‑driven group play; Mountaintop Isolation is streamlined for solo pacing and environmental hazards.

data.title: The End of the World: Wrath of the Gods
data.review_summary: Reviews praise its fast character creation and instantly graspable dice pool with positive/negative dice, while noting that tone drift and group safety tools are important when tackling apocalyptic myths. Scenarios are punchy one‑session arcs; long campaigns need GM scaffolding.
data.blurb: Wrath of the Gods is a rules‑lite modern‑day survival game using a simple positive/negative d6 pool. Classless and low‑prep, it focuses on crisis decisions, scarce resources, and moral tradeoffs as mythic cataclysms unravel society. Ideal for one‑shots or short arcs with story‑first play.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Modern survival • d6 dice pool (positive/negative) • 3–5 + GM • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

Humanity faces apocalypses drawn from living myth—Ragnarök, Cthulhu’s rise, divine wrath, and more. You play yourselves (or close analogs) in your own city as infrastructure fails, gods stride the earth, and familiar places turn hostile. Tone ranges from grounded survival to uncanny horror.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Resolution uses a small pool of positive and negative d6s based on traits, gear, and circumstances. Roll and compare highest matching results to determine success, stress, or complications. Characters are classless, lightly sketched with features and stresses, and advance by hard choices rather than build math. Prep is minimal: each scenario is framed with goals, threats, and branching fallout clocks.

What Makes It Unique

The line’s signature “you as the characters” premise, plus myth‑scale threats in the everyday world, delivers immediacy: your commute, your grocery, your phone battery—all at stake. Scenarios are modular one‑shots with clear beats, making the game easy to table on short notice.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Great for groups who want tense, cinematic survival with light rules and quick rulings. It shines for convention play, pick‑up nights, and short campaigns that emphasize improvisation and consequence. Safety tools and expectation‑setting are recommended to calibrate intensity and themes.

related.title: The End of the World: Zombie Apocalypse
related.description: Shares the same ultra‑light engine and real‑world framing, but swaps divine cataclysm for undead contagion—more grounded logistics and outbreak management versus mythic, reality‑warping threats.

related.title: Mutant: Year Zero
related.description: Both center survival under collapsing systems and hard resource choices; Mutant: Year Zero pushes longer‑form base‑building and hex travel, while Wrath excels at quick, scenario‑driven calamities.

related.title: Those Dark Places
related.description: Each delivers rules‑lite, stress‑forward tension; Those Dark Places leans hard sci‑fi and workplace dread in confined spaces, whereas Wrath roams modern streets as mythologies tear through daily life.

data.title: SURVIVE THIS!! Zombies – Ultimate Edition
data.review_summary: Players praise its plug-and-play OSR chassis and plentiful options for gear, threats, and scenarios. Common caveats note swingy d20 lethality and a toolkit feel that benefits from a GM comfortable curating rules and tone.
data.blurb: SURVIVE THIS!! Zombies is a rules‑lite OSR modern horror game using d20 tests and class‑based play. Low‑prep and modular, it focuses on tense resource management, scavenging, and desperate choices during outbreaks. Ideal for gritty one‑shots or ongoing campaigns about surviving the worst day of your life.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: OSR modern horror • d20 tests • 3–5 + GM • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 2–3h sessions

Theme and Setting

Survive This!! Zombies – Ultimate Edition imagines the first chaotic days and long slog that follow a viral outbreak. Your group decides the tone—from bleak street‑level survival to scrappy neighborhood defense—and zooms in on scarcity, noise discipline, barricades, and the fragile webs of trust that keep people alive. The default is contemporary and grounded, but it flexes to rural, urban, or road‑trip play with equal ease.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Built on an OSR foundation, the game uses familiar ability scores, classes, and d20 resolution with saves and checks. Combat is fast and lethal; complications from noise and injury escalate quickly. Equipment, encumbrance, and ammo tracking matter, but they stay light enough to keep the table moving. Character creation is quick—roll, pick a class, gather a few pieces of gear, and you are ready to face the outbreak in minutes.

What Makes It Unique

The Ultimate Edition compiles and refines content from earlier releases, expanding threats, scenarios, and player options without bloating the core loop. It is intentionally a toolkit: you can run street sweeps, shelter defense, supply runs, or road‑war campaigns just by swapping random tables and encounter procedures. The class list supports distinct roles (first responders, survivalists, academics, everyday civilians) while keeping advancement simple and fast.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Groups who enjoy tense, fiction‑first problem solving will find plenty to love. The rules spotlight planning, stealth, and improvisation rather than build optimization. It plays great as a brutal one‑shot but also sustains longer arcs about territory, hard choices, and the cost of leadership. If you want zombie survival with OSR speed and clarity—roll, decide, move—this delivers.

related.title: The End of the World: Zombie Apocalypse
related.description: Streamlined, scenario‑driven zombie survival with narrative stress mechanics—great when you want fast set‑pieces over OSR resource grind.

related.title: Outbreak: Undead (2nd Edition)
related.description: Simulation‑leaning modern survival that models preparedness and mortality in detail; heavier than ST!! but excellent for grim realism.

related.title: All Flesh Must Be Eaten
related.description: Classic zombie RPG toolkit with countless Deadworlds; crunchier than ST!! yet similarly modular for tailoring tone and threats.

data.title: Dead Reign
data.review_summary: Palladium zombie apocalypse game emphasizes fast, brutal encounters and the stress of survival. Reviews praise its atmosphere and monster variety, while noting swingy combat and legacy crunch from the Palladium system. Best for groups who enjoy tense scenarios over exhaustive character optimization.
data.blurb: Dead Reign is a post-apocalyptic horror RPG about surviving the zombie outbreak. Using a familiar d%/d20 Palladium chassis, it delivers fast, lethal fights, tense scavenging, and hard choices. Class-based, medium-prep, it focuses on teamwork, scarcity, and moral dilemmas. Ideal for campaign play and gritty one-shots.
data.body_html:
At-a-glance: Post-Apocalyptic • d20/d% checks • 3–5 + GM • Medium prep • Moderate crunch • 2–4h sessions

Theme and Setting

Dead Reign drops your table into the raw aftermath of a world-ending zombie outbreak. Cities are fractured, supply lines are gone, and every sound risks drawing a hungry horde. Sessions revolve around food, fuel, and fragile trust—can you protect your people, and what are you willing to sacrifice?

Core Mechanics and Rules

Built on Palladium classic framework, Dead Reign blends d20 attack rolls with d% skills, hit locations, and escalating threats. Combat is fast and dangerous—ammo runs dry, injuries matter, and splitting the party is a recipe for disaster. The rules reward planning and decisive teamwork over lone-wolf heroics.

What Makes It Unique

Instead of treating zombies as background noise, Dead Reign spotlights them: different undead types, behavior cues, and encounter pressures that force tough calls. Scenarios emphasize scavenging routes, barricades, time pressure, and noise management. Survival is not just a theme—it is the loop.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Perfect for groups who want tense, cinematic survival horror with a tactical edge. If you enjoy resource tracking, base defense, and moral crossroads under pressure, Dead Reign delivers. Players looking for ultra-light rules may find it heavier; groups who like a clear, lethal challenge will thrive.

related.title: Mutant: Year Zero
related.description: Shares post-apocalyptic survival and scarcity loops—MYZ leans into push-your-luck dice pools and base building, where Dead Reign emphasizes tense zombie waves and Palladium-style grit.

related.title: All Flesh Must Be Eaten
related.description: Both are zombie survival sandboxes; AFMBE is toolkit-driven with plug-in tone dials, while Dead Reign provides a focused, lethal campaign feel out of the box.

related.title: Outbreak: Undead (2nd Edition)
related.description: Outbreak: Undead models realism and preparedness with robust simulation; Dead Reign moves faster at the table, spotlighting cinematic encounters and horde pressure.

data.title: Ryuutama
data.review_summary: Reviewers praise Ryuutama's wholesome journey focus, elegant travel mechanics, and heartwarming tone reminiscent of Miyazaki films. The seasonal dragon lore and Ryuujin GM role create unique collaborative storytelling. Common caveats: travel rules can feel repetitive, and some crave more random encounter tables for variety.
data.blurb: Ryuutama is a cozy, Japanese-inspired RPG where village folk—bakers, healers, farmers—embark on a grand journey across a world born from seasonal dragons. Classless and heartwarming, it emphasizes travel, camping, and wonder over combat and loot. Ideal for gentle group storytelling and family-friendly tables.
data.body_html:
At‑a‑glance: Natural fantasy • Polyhedral dice • 2–4 + Ryuujin • Low prep • Rules‑lite • 2–4h sessions

Theme and Setting

Ryuutama sends ordinary village folk—minstrels, merchants, artisans—into a world shaped by seasonal dragons. Every landscape, weather pattern, and creature springs from draconic lineage: Spring births growth, Winter births stillness. The tone is heartwarming and wondrous, evoking Studio Ghibli films and gentle JRPGs. Players explore not to conquer but to witness—discovering hot springs, bizarre festivals, and fellow travelers.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Characters choose from mundane professions rather than classes, using four stats (Strength, Dexterity, Intelligence, Spirit) with dice from d4 to d12. All rolls use two relevant stats. The journey itself is the core loop: travel checks, camping procedures, and seasonal events drive play. A unique Ryuujin character—played by the GM—watches over the party, granting blessings and shaping the world through seasonal dragon magic. Combat exists but is de-emphasized; negotiation and preparation matter more.

What Makes It Unique

Ryuutama inverts typical fantasy RPGs: villagers explore while warriors stay home. The travel-check system makes every journey tangible—terrain, weather, and equipment condition affect outcomes. The Ryuujin creates an in-fiction reason for GM intervention that feels collaborative, not adversarial. Most distinct is the tone: it rewards curiosity, kindness, and shared meals around the campfire rather than loot accumulation.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Perfect for groups seeking a palate cleanser from combat-heavy games, families wanting shared creativity, and anyone who has ever wanted to simply go on a journey in a fantasy world. New players grasp the light mechanics quickly; veterans appreciate the subversion of genre expectations. Sessions feel like completing a satisfying chapter—self-contained yet part of a larger, gentle saga.

related.title: Wanderhome
related.description: Both are cozy, non-violent travel RPGs about journey and discovery; Ryuutama uses dice mechanics and seasonal dragons where Wanderhome relies on token economy and kith archetypes.

related.title: Ironsworn
related.description: Shares solo/co-op journey focus and vow-driven play; Ryuutama replaces Ironsworn's grim peril with wholesome exploration and seasonal wonder.

related.title: Quest
related.description: Both offer rules-lite fantasy with approachable mechanics; Ryuutama adds survival travel procedures and gentle JRPG tone versus Quest's class-based ability trees.

data.title: Torchbearer
data.review_summary: A dungeon-crawl survival RPG that emphasizes resource management and the grinding toll of exploration. Players must carefully track light, food, and inventory while navigating dangerous ruins. Reviewers praise the tense atmosphere and meaningful survival mechanics, though note the complexity can be overwhelming for new players.
data.blurb: A survival-focused dungeon crawler where light, food, and supplies deplete constantly. Players must balance risk versus reward as they explore dangerous ruins, with mechanics that emphasize the physical toll of adventuring. Every decision matters when your torch is burning down and you are low on rations.
data.body_html:
At-a-glance: Dungeon survival • Heavy resource management • Burning Wheel lineage • Medium complexity • 3-5 players + GM • 2-4 hour sessions

Theme and Setting

Torchbearer casts players as desperate adventurers exploring dangerous ruins at the edges of civilization. The world is crumbling, pressed by darkness and monsters. Most people huddle behind walls, leaving the brave or foolish to venture into the unknown for treasure and glory.

Core Mechanics and Rules

The game uses a modified Burning Wheel system with heavy emphasis on resource management. Players track light sources, food, water, and equipment degradation. The grind of exploration takes a toll—characters become hungry, thirsty, angry, and afraid. Conflicts use a card-based action selection system where teams script their actions in advance.

What Makes It Unique

Unlike traditional dungeon crawls that focus on combat and loot, Torchbearer emphasizes the logistical challenge of exploration itself. Light is precious, inventory is limited, and every expedition risks disaster. The game creates genuine tension through attrition rather than monster encounters alone.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Ideal for groups who enjoy gritty survival challenges and meaningful resource decisions. The game rewards careful planning and risk assessment. Best for players who appreciate mechanics that reinforce theme, and groups willing to embrace the slow grind of dungeon exploration.

related.title: The Wretched
related.description: Both emphasize survival under pressure with depleting resources; The Wretched is solo horror in space, while Torchbearer is group dungeon survival with complex logistics.

related.title: Mausritter
related.description: Both are inventory-focused survival games with dangerous worlds; Mausritter is rules-lite mouse fantasy, Torchbearer is crunchier human dungeon crawling.

related.title: Index Card RPG
related.description: Both offer streamlined dungeon crawling with an emphasis on quick play and practical mechanics; ICRPG is lighter and more modular, Torchbearer emphasizes survival logistics.

data.title: Cast Away
data.review_summary: Reviewers praise Cast Aways Diminishing Dice mechanic that elegantly models worsening survivor conditions without HP or injury tables. Players appreciate the clean, approachable rules that make it excellent for newcomers and convention play. The Haunting system keeps players engaged even after character death. Common caveats: intentionally minimal condition recovery guidance requires GM interpretation.
data.blurb: Cast Away is a rules-lite survival RPG using Diminishing Dice mechanics to model deteriorating conditions after disaster. Classless and low-prep, it focuses on two stats (Mental and Physical) where dice shrink as survivors tire, starve, or freeze. The Haunting system keeps dead players involved. Ideal for shipwrecks, plane crashes, and wilderness survival one-shots.
data.body_html: At-a-glance: Wilderness survival • Diminishing Dice • 2+ players + Navigator • Low prep • Rules-lite • 2-4h sessions. Cast Away explores harrowing stories of resilience after catastrophic accidents—shipwrecks on uncharted islands, plane crashes in remote wilderness, blizzard entrapment, or urban disasters. The Diminishing Dice system replaces traditional HP. Characters have two stats—Mental and Physical. When survivors take actions under duress, they roll dice that shrink as conditions worsen (d12 to d10 to d8 to d6 to d4). The Haunting system keeps eliminated players engaged as spirits who can influence the living. Perfect for groups wanting approachable survival drama without crunch.

related.title: Endure
related.description: Both Cast Away and Endure deliver rules-lite survival with innovative resource mechanics. Where Endure uses an Endurance economy with 2d6 risk rolls, Cast Away employs Diminishing Dice that physically shrink as conditions worsen. Endure spans more genres; Cast Away focuses tightly on post-disaster wilderness scenarios with its unique Haunting system for eliminated players.

related.title: The Wretched
related.description: Both offer tense solo or small-group survival with minimal prep. The Wretched uses a tumbling block tower for escalating space-horror tension, while Cast Away employs Diminishing Dice for grounded wilderness scenarios. Both embrace inevitable doom as a storytelling device.

related.title: Ten Candles
related.description: Both Cast Away and Ten Candles use physical, tangible mechanics to model dwindling hope—Ten Candles with its literal countdown to darkness, Cast Away with shrinking dice. Ten Candles leans ritual and tragic horror; Cast Away offers more survival problem-solving and recovery possibility.

data.title: Durance
data.review_summary: A prison planet survival game emphasizing brutal hierarchy and hard choices. Reviewers praise the collaborative storytelling and Fiasco-like mechanics adapted for sci-fi survival. Common note: best for groups comfortable with player-vs-player tension.
data.blurb: On a remote prison planet, convicts and guards struggle to survive in a brutal hierarchy. This fast-paced, collaborative game emphasizes hard choices and conflicting codes of conduct as everyone fights for limited resources and power.
data.body_html:
At-a-glance: Prison planet survival • Fiasco-inspired • 3-5 players • Low prep • 2-3 hour sessions • Sci-fi

Theme and Setting

On a remote planet far from civilization, the worst criminal scum from a dozen star systems have been dumped, charged with building new lives under the watchful eye of Authority. Within a brutal hierarchy of savagery and servility, convicts and guards alike must make hard choices.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Durance uses a modified version of the Fiasco system adapted for survival storytelling. Players establish relationships, needs, and locations through collaborative setup, then play out scenes that escalate tensions. The game includes specific mechanics for the brutal prison environment and shifting power dynamics.

What Makes It Unique

The prison planet setting creates natural tension between survival and morality. Every colonist has their own code of conduct and aspirations that come at the expense of others. The game forces players to navigate impossible choices in a world too small for everyone to succeed.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Ideal for groups who enjoy collaborative storytelling with inter-player tension. Best for players comfortable with moral ambiguity and conflict. The low-prep design makes it easy to run, but the subject matter requires a mature group willing to explore dark themes.

related.title: The Wretched
related.description: Both are survival games in hostile environments with depleting resources; The Wretched is solo horror aboard a ship, Durance is group survival in a prison colony.

related.title: Stay Frosty
related.description: Both are sci-fi survival games with external threats; Stay Frosty is military action against bugs, Durance is political survival among convicts and guards.

related.title: Zombie World
related.description: Both are survival games about communities under pressure using collaborative mechanics; Zombie World uses cards for emergent stories, Durance uses Fiasco-style scene framing.

data.title: Twilight: 2000
data.review_summary: A gritty survival RPG that won the Judges Award for Best RPG at UK Games Expo 2022.
