Written by RPGPundit, it takes the foundational mechanics of early D&D and reimagines them through a lens of historical authenticity rather than pulp fantasy. The result is a grittier, more grounded take on medieval fantasy that emphasizes social hierarchy, economic realism, and historically-inspired magic.

Theme and Setting

The game presents a default setting that draws heavily from medieval European history, particularly the feudal period. Unlike traditional fantasy RPGs that mix Tolkienesque elements with medieval window dressing, Lion & Dragon commits to historical authenticity. Social class matters deeply—a peasant talking back to a noble faces real consequences, and knights operate within a rigid feudal system rather than as wandering mercenaries. The implied setting emphasizes civilization as survival, with the settled lands representing islands of safety amid dangerous wilderness. This creates a fundamentally different dynamic than typical dungeon-crawling OSR games, where adventurers are rootless outsiders.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Lion & Dragon runs on OSR-compatible mechanics familiar to players of original D&D and its retroclones. Characters have the standard six ability scores, classes include Fighters, Clerics, and Magic-Users, and combat uses d20 attack rolls against ascending armor class. However, the system introduces significant modifications to support its medieval authentic approach.

Fighters receive multiple attacks against weaker enemies, making them feel genuinely powerful in ways that reflect real martial superiority. Healing is slow and limited—magical healing does not function as a convenient reset button, meaning wounds matter and every combat carries lasting consequences. The equipment and economic systems are deeply integrated with social standing; characters cannot simply purchase whatever gear they can afford. Instead, access to certain items depends on social class and status, making wealth and possessions meaningful roleplaying elements rather than mere mechanics.

What Makes It Unique

The magic system stands as Lion & Dragon's most distinctive feature. Rather than the fireball-slinging wizards of high fantasy, magic in this game draws from historical grimoires and actual medieval occult practices. Alchemy, summoning, and astrology replace convenient spell slots and flashy evocations. Magic is risky, complicated, and dangerous—exactly as medieval practitioners believed it to be.

The game's commitment to 'medieval authentic' play extends to its treatment of law, justice, and economics. The rules include detailed sections on medieval trade, feudal obligations, and legal systems. Monsters and magical items derive from authentic medieval legends rather than modern fantasy tropes. This creates a world that feels genuinely historical while still providing the wonder and danger that make fantasy RPGs compelling.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Lion & Dragon serves groups seeking a deeper, more immersive medieval experience than standard fantasy RPGs provide. It rewards players willing to engage with social hierarchy and historical context rather than treating them as obstacles to dungeon delving. The game excels at campaigns where political maneuvering, social climbing, and careful resource management matter as much as combat prowess.

OSR veterans will appreciate the mechanical compatibility with existing material while finding fresh ground in the historical approach. Groups transitioning from more modern systems should expect an adjustment period—the slower healing, limited magic, and social constraints require different tactical and strategic thinking. The game is not designed for quick one-shots but rather rewards long-term campaign play where characters build reputations, establish holdings, and navigate the complex web of feudal obligations.
data.notes: Published by DOM Publishing in 2017. Written by RPGPundit. Companion setting Dark Albion available. Magic system based on historical occult practices.

related.title: The Hero's Journey
related.description: Another OSR game that reimagines classic D&D through a specific thematic lens, The Hero's Journey focuses on mythic fantasy and epic storytelling. Where Lion & Dragon emphasizes historical medieval authenticity, The Hero's Journey embraces folklore and legend. Both games share OSR mechanical compatibility while offering distinctly different campaign experiences.

related.title: Beyond the Wall
related.description: Beyond the Wall offers a different approach to grounded fantasy, focusing on small-scale heroism in a fairy-tale inspired setting. While Lion & Dragon commits to historical medieval Europe, Beyond the Wall draws from folklore and village-level adventure. Both games emphasize character connections to their communities and the dangers of the wilderness beyond civilization.

related.title: Swords & Wizardry
related.description: Swords & Wizardry provides a clean, compatible foundation for OSR play that works well as a mechanical reference for Lion & Dragon. While Swords & Wizardry presents a more traditional fantasy approach, its streamlined rules make it easy to port material between systems. Groups wanting to add more traditional fantasy elements to Lion & Dragon can do so seamlessly.

data.title: Sharp Swords & Sinister Spells
data.review_summary: A lightweight OSR sword-and-sorcery RPG with a unified d20 mechanic, three archetypes (Warrior, Specialist, Magic User), and a dangerous magic system where spellcasting risks corruption and backfire. Pay-what-you-want pricing makes it highly accessible.
data.blurb: A rules-lite OSR RPG built for sword-and-sorcery adventures in the tradition of Robert E. Howard and Conan. Features a unified d20 roll-under mechanic, three archetypes with customizable vocations and background complications, plus dangerous magic that risks corruption on failure. Character creation is fast and includes Luck Dice that degrade with misfortune. Combat emphasizes agility and armor damage reduction. Pay-what-you-want pricing makes this an excellent entry point for OSR newcomers.
data.body_html:
Sharp Swords & Sinister Spells is a rules-lite OSR RPG designed specifically for sword-and-sorcery adventures in the tradition of Robert E. Howard, Lin Carter, and Karl Edward Wagner. Created by Diogo Nogueira of Old Skull Publishing and first released in 2016, it delivers fast-paced pulp action with minimal mechanical overhead while maintaining enough depth to support extended campaigns.

Theme and Setting

The game evokes a dying world of decadent city-states surrounded by savage wilderness and ancient ruins where great kingdoms once stood. This is humanocentric fantasy where brave adventurers seek glory, gold, and forbidden knowledge against a backdrop of corruption, dark sorcery, and treacherous intrigue. The tone emphasizes pulp action over high fantasy heroism—characters are rogues, barbarians, and ambitious spellcasters motivated by personal gain and survival rather than noble causes. Magic is dangerous and corrupting, monsters are deadly threats rather than experience point fodder, and civilization clings to existence in the face of encroaching chaos.

Core Mechanics

SS&SS uses a unified d20 roll-under mechanic that keeps play flowing quickly. Characters have four attributes—Physique, Agility, Intellect, and Willpower—determined by rolling 3d6 in order. To attempt any action, players roll d20 and aim to score equal to or under the relevant attribute. The system includes a Luck Die mechanic representing fortune that degrades when players roll poorly (1-2), eventually running out entirely and leaving characters at the mercy of fate.

Combat tests attributes directly—Physique for melee attacks and Agility for ranged combat. Armor reduces incoming damage rather than making characters harder to hit, creating tactical decisions about protection versus mobility. Initiative is determined by Hit Die size, with heavily armored Warriors acting before lighter combatants. Healing is intentionally limited with no magical restoration available; characters must find safe havens for Long Rests to recover hit dice, making wilderness exploration genuinely perilous.

Character Creation

Players choose from three Archetypes that define their approach to adventure. Warriors receive d10 Hit Dice and excel at combat, wielding any weapon and wearing any armor. Specialists use d8 Hit Dice and focus on skills, stealth, and precision, making them ideal for thieves, scouts, and assassins. Magic Users have d6 Hit Dice but command sorcerous power, though at great personal risk.

Each character selects a Vocation representing their background and concept—a Warrior might be a Berserker or Gladiator, while a Specialist could be an Artificer or Spy. Players also roll for a Complication that adds narrative hooks: perhaps the character is addicted to lotus powder, hunted by a chaos cult, or owes debts to a corrupt noble. These elements create immediate story potential and distinguish characters beyond their statistics.

Magic System

Magic in SS&SS is powerful but perilous, drawing inspiration from Dungeon Crawl Classics. Spellcasters test Willpower to successfully cast; failure risks losing the spell for the day or accepting a Referee-imposed complication such as corruption, mutation, or attracting unwanted supernatural attention. Unlike traditional Vancian systems, all fifty spells are available from the beginning with no level restrictions.

Casters choose Power Level when casting, with higher power creating more spectacular effects but making success more difficult. This creates meaningful tactical tension between magical ambition and survival. The spell list includes classic sword-and-sorcery effects like conjuration, transmutation, and enthrallment, all described briefly to encourage creative interpretation by players and Referees.

Target Audience

Sharp Swords & Sinister Spells is ideal for players seeking lightweight OSR play with authentic sword-and-sorcery flavor. The game suits groups wanting quick character creation, fast combat resolution, and dangerous magic without complex rules or extensive preparation. Pay-what-you-want pricing makes it highly accessible for curious players and gaming groups on a budget.

The system works best for Referees comfortable with making rulings rather than consulting detailed rules, and for players who enjoy pulp action, moral ambiguity, and high-stakes adventure over tactical combat depth. Groups that appreciate the aesthetic of Conan, Elric, or Fafhrd and the Gray Mouser will find the tone perfectly calibrated to those classics. The included adventure generator provides immediate tools for creating scenarios, making this a complete package for getting swords-and-sorcery campaigns started quickly.

related.title: Dungeon Crawl Classics
related.description: Dungeon Crawl Classics is a major OSR RPG featuring a unique dice chain mechanic and the iconic funnel system where players run multiple zero-level peasants through deadly trials. Published by Goodman Games with an extensive module library supporting old-school dungeon crawling.

related.title: Mörk Borg
related.description: Mörk Borg is a pitch-black apocalyptic fantasy RPG with doom metal aesthetics. Winner of four ENNIE Awards including Product of the Year 2020. Features rules-light mechanics, frequent character death, and a dying world setting where the end is inevitable.

related.title: Whitehack
related.description: Whitehack is an innovative OSR RPG with a unique class system and flexible magic. Features three classes—Deft, Strong, and Wise—with open-ended vocation mechanics. Known for its hackable design and strong following among OSR enthusiasts.

data.title: For Gold & Glory
data.review_summary: Reviewers praise For Gold & Glory as the definitive AD&D 2e retroclone, faithfully capturing the system's feel while improving organization. The consolidation of PHB, DMG, and MM content into one volume receives particular appreciation, as does the free PDF availability. Some note the THAC0 system and percentile skills require adjustment for players accustomed to modern mechanics, but veterans find it delivers authentic 2e nostalgia.
data.blurb: For Gold & Glory is a faithful retroclone of AD&D 2nd Edition, consolidating the Player's Handbook, DMG treasure sections, and Monster Manual into a single comprehensive tome. It preserves the classic THAC0 system, percentile skills, and race-class combinations while cleaning up ambiguities through decades of community playtesting. Perfect for those seeking authentic 2e nostalgia without tracking down vintage books.
data.body_html:
At-a-glance: AD&D 2e retroclone • d20 THAC0 + percentile skills • 2-8 + GM • Low prep • Rules Medium • 3-4h sessions

Theme and Setting

For Gold & Glory embraces the classic high-fantasy kitchen-sink setting that defined Advanced Dungeons & Dragons 2nd Edition. The system supports everything from grim dungeon crawls to epic planar adventures, with mechanics that assume a world of human-centric civilizations surrounded by dangerous wilderness and ancient ruins. Unlike more focused modern OSR games, this retroclone preserves the anything-goes approach of the 1990s—dragon-riding knights, planar travelers, and dungeon-delving thieves all fit comfortably within its framework. The bestiary includes classic monsters from aboleths to zombies, while the magic system supports the full spectrum of D&D spellcasting from fireballs to wish spells.

Core Mechanics and Rules

The game uses the familiar d20-based THAC0 system for combat, where lower armor class is better and attack rolls must exceed a target number derived from the attacker's skill versus defender's protection. Percentile dice (d100) handle thief skills and other specialized abilities, creating a dual-resolution system that feels distinctly 2e. Character creation follows the classic race and class separation—humans, elves, dwarves, halflings, gnomes, and half-elves each have access to different class combinations, with prime requisite stats affecting experience point bonuses. The book consolidates what originally required three separate volumes: 94 pages of core rules, followed by 105 pages of magic, treasure, and monsters. Weapon proficiency groups and optional humanoid character rules from the Complete series appear as integrated elements rather than later add-ons.

What Makes It Unique

Where For Gold & Glory distinguishes itself is in its meticulous attention to rules clarity. The author compiled the text from original AD&D sources, then back-filled gaps using Basic D&D and Dragon magazine, finally resolving remaining ambiguities through Sage Advice columns and Dragonsforum community wisdom. The result plays like the heavily road-tested version of 2e that evolved at kitchen tables over decades—familiar to veterans but more accessible than deciphering vintage rulebooks. The single-volume format eliminates the need for multiple books at the table, while the print-on-demand availability makes this slice of gaming history accessible without hunting eBay. Public domain art from Walter Crane and others gives the book a distinctive aesthetic that evokes the period without directly copying trademarked imagery.

Target Audience and Player Experience

This game serves two primary audiences: veterans who played 2e in the 1990s and want to recapture that specific mechanical feel, and newcomers curious about the OSR who want to understand what made AD&D distinct from its Basic and Original predecessors. The learning curve is moderate—more complex than B/X clones like Old-School Essentials but less baroque than trying to parse original AD&D's organization. Groups comfortable with THAC0 and percentile systems will find the game runs smoothly, with enough class options to satisfy players who want mechanical distinction without the build complexity of 3e or 5e. The system particularly shines for campaign play, supporting characters from level 1 through the high teens with meaningful progression and the full range of classic D&D challenges.
data.notes: AD&D 2e retroclone. Free PDF available on DriveThruRPG.

related.title: OSRIC
related.description: OSRIC is the original AD&D 1e retroclone, while For Gold & Glory covers 2nd Edition. Both offer faithful recreations of advanced D&D rules, but FG&G includes more integrated options from the Complete series and a more modern single-book presentation.

related.title: Old-School Essentials
related.description: Old-School Essentials streamlines Basic/Expert D&D with exceptional organization, while For Gold & Glory preserves the more complex AD&D 2e system with its race-class separation and THAC0 mechanics. Choose OSE for simplicity or FG&G for 2e authenticity.

related.title: Castles & Crusades
related.description: Castles & Crusades modernizes classic D&D with the Siege Engine mechanic, bridging old-school feel with contemporary design. For Gold & Glory stays truer to original 2e mechanics, making it ideal for purists seeking unfiltered AD&D experience.

data.title: Shadow of the Weird Wizard
data.review_summary: Shadow of the Weird Wizard delivers a polished evolution of the Demon Lord engine, trading nihilistic horror for heroic fantasy while retaining mechanical depth. The three-path character system and boon/bane dice mechanic create satisfying progression, though some players note the layout and graphic design feel less evocative than the dark predecessor.
data.blurb: A fantasy RPG by Robert J. Schwalb using an evolved Shadow of the Demon Lord system. Features d20 mechanics with boons/banes, three-path character progression, and heroic quests to protect refugees in the borderlands. Designed for 10-quest campaigns with scaling complexity from simple beginnings to epic finales.
data.body_html:
Shadow of the Weird Wizard is a fantasy roleplaying game by Robert J. Schwalb (designer of Shadow of the Demon Lord and D&D 5e contributor) that evolves the Demon Lord engine into a heroic, family-friendly direction. Players assume roles of heroes protecting refugees in the borderlands—lands once ruled by the mysterious Weird Wizard, now haunted by monsters, cruel faeries, undead, and ancient evils.

Theme and Setting

The game presents a world at a pivotal moment: the old order has collapsed into war and chaos, driving refugees from the Old Country into the lightly-populated borderlands. Unlike its predecessor's apocalyptic nihilism, Weird Wizard embraces heroic questing where characters forge alliances, explore ruins left by trolls and faeries, and protect the displaced as they rebuild their lives. The tone balances dark fantasy elements with hope—dangerous enough to feel consequential, but heroic enough for uplifting narratives.

Core Mechanics

The system uses a unified d20 core mechanic with boons and banes (d6s) that mitigate the swinginess of the die. Character creation is rapid, allowing play to begin quickly. The three-path progression system lets characters develop through Novice, Expert, and Master paths, enabling combinations like a cleric-turned-mountebank-turned-alienist. Campaigns are structured around ten quests, each completable in a few hours, with scaling complexity that makes early play approachable and late-game play exciting.

What Makes It Unique

Shadow of the Weird Wizard stands out for its deliberate tonal shift from Demon Lord's grimdark apocalypse to accessible heroic fantasy without sacrificing mechanical sophistication. The boon/bane system adds tactical texture to d20 rolls. The three-path character system offers deep customization while maintaining clarity. Most notably, the game is designed for shorter campaigns—ten quests total—making it ideal for groups who want complete narratives without multi-year commitments.

Target Audience

This game suits players seeking heroic fantasy with modern mechanical sensibilities, groups wanting complete campaigns in manageable timeframes, and Demon Lord fans wanting a lighter tone. It's particularly appealing to 5e players curious about OSR-style design with more narrative flexibility, and to GMs who value structured campaign arcs with clear endpoints.

related.title: 13th Age
related.description: Both Shadow of the Weird Wizard and 13th Age modernize d20 fantasy with narrative-focused innovations. While 13th Age uses Icons and "One Unique Thing" to embed characters in its world, Weird Wizard employs a three-path progression system and boon/bane mechanics. 13th Age leans into high fantasy heroics with abstract combat, while Weird Wizard maintains tighter mechanical coupling to its borderlands setting and refugee-protection themes.

related.title: ShadowDark
related.description: ShadowDark and Shadow of the Weird Wizard both bridge traditional and modern RPG design, but approach fantasy from different angles. ShadowDark emphasizes real-time tension with torch timers and deadly dungeon crawling, while Weird Wizard focuses on heroic quest structure and character path customization. ShadowDark's randomized progression contrasts with Weird Wizard's deliberate three-path system, offering different flavors of streamlined fantasy.

data.title: Legend in the Mist
data.notes: Urban fantasy RPG about myths in the modern world. Added by request.

data.title: Cosmere RPG
data.review_summary: The Cosmere RPG receives praise for its faithful adaptation of Brandon Sanderson's intricate magic systems and its innovative "plot die" mechanic that weaves narrative directly into gameplay. Reviewers appreciate the flexible character progression through talent trees and the skill-based magic approach, though some note the rules can feel complex for newcomers. The collaboration between Brotherwise Games and Sanderson himself ensures authentic lore integration across the Stormlight Archive and Mistborn settings.
data.blurb: The official RPG for Brandon Sanderson's Cosmere universe featuring Stormlight Archive and Mistborn settings. Uses a d20-based system with talent trees, skill-based magic, and a unique "plot die" that raises narrative stakes. Players can create Knights Radiant, Mistborn, or worldhoppers traversing multiple realms.
data.body_html:
At-a-glance: d20 mechanics • Talent trees • Skill-based magic • Plot die narrative system • Stormlight & Mistborn settings • Worldhopping campaigns • Official Brandon Sanderson collaboration

Theme and Setting

The Cosmere RPG brings Brandon Sanderson's interconnected fantasy universe to the tabletop, spanning the storm-swept world of Roshar (Stormlight Archive) and the ash-covered realm of Scadrial (Mistborn). Players inhabit a galaxy where unique magic systems operate on distinct worlds, connected by the mysterious Cognitive Realm that allows worldhopping between planets.

On Roshar, players can become Knights Radiant, forming bonds with spren to wield Shardblades and harness Stormlight. On Scadrial, characters might be Allomancers burning metals for power, Feruchemists storing attributes in metalminds, or Hemalurgists wielding stolen abilities. The setting embraces Sanderson's signature hard magic systems—powers with clear rules, costs, and limitations that drive storytelling.

Core Mechanics

The Cosmere RPG utilizes a familiar d20-based resolution system while introducing purpose-built innovations that distinguish it from traditional fantasy RPGs:

The Plot Die

A special d6 mechanic that activates during critical story moments. When the GM calls to "raise the stakes," players roll the plot die alongside their d20. Results can grant Opportunities (resources for dramatic turns), introduce complications, or escalate the narrative tension. This mechanic directly ties mechanical outcomes to story development.

Talent Trees

Character progression uses branching talent trees rather than rigid classes. Players customize their abilities by selecting talents that reflect their character's unique path—whether developing Surgebinding powers as a Knight Radiant or mastering different Allomantic metals as a Mistborn.

Skill-Based Magic

Magic isn't automatic—it requires skill checks to activate. This creates risk and drama around using powers, with consequences for failure that vary by magic system. Stormlight users might leak precious light; Allomancers might burn metals inefficiently.

Action Economy

Combat uses an action point system where players have 2-3 points per turn to spend on Actions (1-3 points each), Reactions, or Free Actions. This creates tactical depth without grid-based complexity.

What Makes It Unique

Several elements distinguish the Cosmere RPG from other licensed properties:

  • Living RPG: Designed to expand as Sanderson releases new novels, with content planned for future Cosmere worlds.
  • Authentic Magic Systems: Faithfully recreates Sanderson's scientifically-grounded magic with proper limitations, costs, and interactions.
  • Worldhopping Campaigns: Rules support campaigns that move between different worlds and magic systems—a feature rare in tabletop RPGs.
  • Narrative Integration: The plot die mechanic ensures story consequences matter mechanically, not just narratively.
  • Official Collaboration: Developed with direct input from Brandon Sanderson and Dragonsteel Entertainment.

Target Audience

The Cosmere RPG appeals to:

  • Sanderson Fans: Readers eager to roleplay in worlds they love with authentic mechanics.
  • d20 Players: Those seeking familiar mechanics with fresh narrative innovations.
  • Magic System Enthusiasts: Players who enjoy hard magic with clear rules and creative applications.
  • Narrative-Driven Groups: Those wanting mechanics that reinforce story development.
  • Setting Hoppers: GMs interested in multi-world campaigns with distinct magical cultures.

The game requires investment in learning its systems, but rewards players with deep, lore-authentic experiences in one of fantasy's most meticulously constructed universes.
data.notes: Official RPG for Brandon Sanderson's Cosmere universe (Mistborn, Stormlight Archive). Added by request.

related.title: Dungeons & Dragons
related.description: Dungeons & Dragons and Cosmere RPG both utilize d20-based mechanics and class-like character progression, but diverge significantly in their approach to magic and narrative integration. While D&D offers a broad toolkit of spells and abilities applicable across generic fantasy settings, Cosmere RPG focuses on deeply integrated, lore-specific magic systems with strict rules and limitations that drive storytelling. For example, Cosmere's "plot die" mechanic weaves narrative stakes directly into resolution, whereas D&D typically separates mechanics from story consequences.

related.title: 13th Age
related.description: 13th Age and Cosmere RPG share a common foundation in d20 mechanics while both emphasizing narrative-driven gameplay innovations. However, 13th Age focuses on collaborative worldbuilding through its Icon relationship system and abstract combat, while Cosmere RPG delivers a meticulously crafted, canon-rich setting with hard magic systems that have specific mechanical costs and limitations. For instance, both use familiar d20 resolution, but Cosmere's skill-based magic requires rolls to activate powers, whereas 13th Age's abilities typically activate automatically.

related.title: Avatar Legends
related.description: Avatar Legends and Cosmere RPG are both licensed properties that translate beloved fictional magic systems into tabletop mechanics with narrative focus. While Avatar Legends uses the Powered by the Apocalypse framework for collaborative, fluid storytelling centered on elemental bending and character balance, Cosmere RPG employs a more traditional d20 structure with precise magic rules and tactical combat. For example, Avatar Legends emphasizes emotional states and group dynamics through its PbtA moves, whereas Cosmere RPG focuses on the strategic application of hard magic systems with clear costs and limitations.

data.title: Triangle Agency
data.review_summary: Winner of the 2025 Gold ENNIE Awards for Best Game, Best Rules, and Best Writing. Polygon called it 'the best game I've ever read,' praising its astronomical punchline-per-page ratio and comedic dissection of corporate culture. Bloody Disgusting described it as 'a special game' with compelling characters and creative problem-solving mechanics.
data.blurb: A satirical horror RPG about wielding reality-warping powers while navigating corporate bureaucracy. Winner of multiple ENNIE awards, it blends chilling horror with dark comedy as players investigate supernatural Anomalies for a mysterious Agency. Features 729 character combinations and unlockable 'Playwalled' secrets.
data.body_html:
Triangle Agency is a satirical horror tabletop role-playing game about employees at a secretive and oppressive corporation. Players take on the role of Field Agents working for the Triangle Agency, an international corporation with influence in every industry, tasked with investigating and capturing supernatural Anomalies that threaten normal citizens.

Theme and Setting

The game draws inspiration from Control (video game), House of Leaves (novel), The Matrix (film), and The X-Files (TV series). The setting is a world where supernatural entities called Anomalies constantly threaten everyday life. The Triangle Agency operates as a shadowy corporate entity that controls information, manipulates reality, and expects absolute loyalty from its employees. The tone seamlessly transitions between chilling horror, wacky comedy, and the emotional truth of trying to survive in a world with no right answers. The game explores themes of late-stage capitalism, corporate oppression, and the cost of power.

Core Mechanics and Rules

Triangle Agency uses the all-new Stability/Chaos rule system. Players roll a pool of six four-sided dice (d4s); the roll is a success if the pool contains at least one 3. When players fail rolls, the GM gains Chaos points to spend on powerful effects and obstacles. Character creation uses the unique ARC system with 729 possible starting combinations of Anomaly (supernatural powers), Reality (everyday obligations and relationships), and Competency (assigned Agency role). The session structure is based on procedural dramas, with each mission focusing on dealing with uncontained Anomalies or eliminating evidence of their existence. The book includes over 100 pages of secret material called the Playwalled Documents that unlock through gameplay.

What Makes It Unique

The game features a metatextual instruction book written in the style of a company handbook, with multiple unreliable narrators. According to the in-fiction text, the players are actually employees at the corporation hallucinating their real lives. This corporate horror genre creates a distinct experience that satirizes workplace culture while delivering genuine scares. The game won the 2025 Gold ENNIE Awards for Best Game, Best Rules, and Best Writing, plus the 2025 Golden Fez Award for Best Design and Production. The 300-page fully illustrated book combines gorgeous art with slick graphic design that reinforces the corporate aesthetic.

Target Audience and Player Experience

Designed for 2-5 players plus a GM (called the General Manager), Triangle Agency works for campaigns of 10-30 missions or high-energy one-shots. The game appeals to players who enjoy horror with humor, fans of corporate dystopias, and groups looking for innovative mechanics that tie directly to theme. The rulebook is praised for being one of the funniest TTRPG rulebooks while maintaining deep playability. Players who enjoy Control, The X-Files, or workplace satires will find familiar ground here. The game requires six four-sided dice and includes form-fillable and printable character sheets.
data.notes: Bureaucratic horror about working for a mysterious agency. Added by request.

related.title: Delta Green
related.description: Both games feature agents working for secretive organizations investigating supernatural threats. While Delta Green focuses on cosmic horror and the sanity-shattering truth of the Cthulhu Mythos, Triangle Agency leans into corporate satire and bureaucratic horror. Delta Green uses percentile-based mechanics and emphasizes psychological deterioration, whereas Triangle Agency employs its Stability/Chaos system with a focus on workplace dynamics and dark humor. Choose Delta Green for bleak, realistic horror; choose Triangle Agency for horror with comedic bite.

related.title: Monster of the Week
related.description: Both games put players in the role of investigators hunting supernatural threats in a modern setting. Monster of the Week uses the Powered by the Apocalypse framework for episodic monster hunting inspired by shows like Buffy and Supernatural. Triangle Agency offers a more structured corporate framework with its unique ARC character system and unlockable Playwalled content. Monster of the Week is more improvisational and GM-flexible, while Triangle Agency provides extensive built-in setting material and a defined progression system. Both excel at TV-style procedural storytelling.

related.title: FIST: Ultra Edition
related.description: Both games feature paranormal operatives working within organized structures to contain supernatural threats. FIST takes a rules-lite, military approach to paranormal mercenary work with quick character creation and mission-based play. Triangle Agency offers a more robust 300-page system with deeper character customization (729 combinations) and a metatextual corporate handbook presentation. FIST is ideal for quick one-shots and action-focused play, while Triangle Agency supports longer campaigns with its unlockable secrets and evolving narrative. Both share a love for strange powers and dangerous missions.

data.title: Fantasy
data.description: A genre featuring magical elements, mythical creatures, and imaginative worlds, often set in medieval-like settings.
data.body_html: A genre featuring magical elements, mythical creatures, and imaginative worlds, often set in medieval-like settings.

data.title: High-Fantasy
data.description: A subgenre of fantasy characterized by epic tales in entirely fictional worlds, with grand themes of good versus evil.
data.body_html: A subgenre of fantasy characterized by epic tales in entirely fictional worlds, with grand themes of good versus evil.

data.title: Dark Fantasy
data.description: Combines fantasy elements with horror, presenting grim settings, morally ambiguous characters, and themes of despair.
data.body_html: Combines fantasy elements with horror, presenting grim settings, morally ambiguous characters, and themes of despair.

data.title: Science Fiction
data.description: Explores futuristic concepts, advanced technology, space exploration, and often delves into the impact of science on society.
data.body_html: Explores futuristic concepts, advanced technology, space exploration, and often delves into the impact of science on society.

data.title: Cyberpunk
data.description: A subgenre of science fiction set in dystopian futures, highlighting advanced technology, cybernetics, and societal decay.
data.body_html: A subgenre of science fiction set in dystopian futures, highlighting advanced technology, cybernetics, and societal decay.

data.title: Post-Apocalyptic
data.description: Depicts worlds after catastrophic events, focusing on survival in environments where civilization has collapsed.
data.body_html: Depicts worlds after catastrophic events, focusing on survival in environments where civilization has collapsed.

data.title: Horror
data.description: A genre designed to evoke fear, featuring supernatural elements, monsters, or psychological terror.
data.body_html: A genre designed to evoke fear, featuring supernatural elements, monsters, or psychological terror.

data.title: Cosmic Horror
data.description: Centers on the insignificance of humanity in the vast universe, often involving incomprehensible, malevolent entities.
data.body_html: Centers on the insignificance of humanity in the vast universe, often involving incomprehensible, malevolent entities.

data.title: Urban Fantasy
data.description: Blends magical elements with modern urban settings, where supernatural beings coexist with everyday life.

data.title: Rules Lite
data.description: Rules‑light games keep mechanics minimal so you can get to the table fast and stay in the story.
data.body_html: Rules‑light (aka rules‑lite) TTRPGs cut bookkeeping and edge cases so you can play sooner and focus on fiction. Expect: streamlined character creation, simple resolution (often 1–2 dice), and minimal modifiers. Great for one‑shots, new players, and tables that prefer rulings over rules. They’re not just ‘simple’—many are razor‑sharp by design (OSR/NSR titles, narrative engines, and modern indie systems). Browse below for quick starts and deeper picks across fantasy, horror, sci‑fi, and more.

data.title: 5e Compatible
data.description: Games that are compatible with or based on Dungeons & Dragons 5th Edition mechanics. These systems use the d20 core, similar class structures, and can often use 5e modules and supplements with minimal conversion.
data.body_html:
What is 5e Compatible?

"5e Compatible" games are tabletop RPGs that use the core mechanics of Dungeons & Dragons 5th Edition (D&D 5e) or are designed to work seamlessly with 5e content. This includes:

  • Direct compatibility – Games that use the same d20 system, proficiency bonuses, and core mechanics as 5e
  • 5e-based systems – Games that build on 5e foundations with additional rules or modifications
  • OSR/retro-clones – Old-School Renaissance games that share DNA with early D&D editions and can often use 5e material

Why Play 5e Compatible Games?

Familiar Mechanics: If you know 5e, you already know most of the rules. Learning curve is minimal.

Content Compatibility: Use your existing 5e modules, monsters, and magic items with little or no conversion.

Variety Within Comfort: Experience different settings, genres, and playstyles while keeping the mechanics you enjoy.

Community Size: 5e is the most popular TTRPG, so finding players and resources is easy.
